title: Sticky ginger loaf
subtitle: Dark, treacly loaf cake with ground ginger and black treacle, keeps for a week.
excerpt: Sticky ginger loaf is a dark, dense slab cake made with treacle and golden syrup for a deep, almost smoky sweetness and a good dose of ground ginger. It is the kind of cake that improves with keeping: moist and sticky on day one, even better on day three. Scatter a few pieces of chopped crystallised ginger on top before baking if you have them.
sourceNotes: Gingerbread and ginger loaves are among the oldest forms of spiced cake in British and Northern European cooking. Parkin, a Yorkshire variant using oats, is documented from the eighteenth century. The treacle-based loaf cake form became widely popular in Britain through the nineteenth century and appears in household manuals of that period. This recipe follows the classic dark treacle-and-syrup loaf method, closely related to the gingerbread described in Isabella Beeton's 'Book of Household Management' (1861). The addition of crystallised ginger as a topping is a later variant, optional here.

content.text: Sticky ginger loaf relies on black treacle and golden syrup, not just sugar, for its dark character. The treacle brings a bitter depth; the golden syrup adds a particular British sweetness that is different from honey or plain sugar. Together they produce a cake that is almost treacle-dark and keeps for a week without drying out.

The method is: melt the wet ingredients together in a pan, add the dry ingredients, then the eggs and milk. No creaming, no electric mixer. If you have crystallised ginger to hand, scatter a small handful of chopped pieces over the batter before baking; they sink slightly into the surface and add a candied crunch.
Heat the oven to 160°C fan (180°C conventional). Grease and line a 20 x 30 cm rectangular tin with baking paper.

Melt {{unsalted-butter}}, {{black-treacle}}, {{golden-syrup}}, and {{light-brown-sugar}} together in a medium saucepan over a low heat, stirring until the butter melts and the sugar dissolves. Remove from the heat and leave to cool for 5 minutes.
Sift {{plain-flour}}, {{ginger-ground}}, {{mixed-spice}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} into a large bowl. Add {{bicarbonate-of-soda}} and stir it in well.
Pour the warm treacle mixture into the flour and stir until smooth. Add {{eggs}} and {{whole-milk}} and stir again. The batter will be quite liquid.

Pour into the prepared tin. Scatter a small handful of finely chopped crystallised ginger over the surface if you have it. Bake for 40 to 50 minutes until just set in the centre and a skewer comes out clean.
Leave to cool completely in the tin before cutting. The cake will feel very moist and sticky; it firms up as it cools. Wrap any leftovers in the baking paper and store in a tin at room temperature.

attrs.intro: This is a forgiving cake, but the liquid batter can catch people off guard.

items.fix: Cover loosely with foil and continue in 10-minute intervals. A 900 g loaf tin needs 55 to 65 minutes; a shallow rectangular tin needs 40 to 45 minutes.
items.cause: Tin too deep, or oven running cool.

items.fix: Move the tin to the middle rack. Brush the top with a little extra treacle thinned with warm water before baking if it looks pale. The flavour will not suffer.
items.cause: Light-coloured tin reflecting heat, or oven position too low.
items.symptom: Cake very pale on top despite being baked through.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
